common_name: Cherokee Brave Dogwood (Deep Red Flowering Dogwood)

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container trees in late winter or early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade; Cherokee Brave requires excellent drainage and partial shade in zone 9 — morning sun with afternoon shade prevents the leaf scorch and stress that shortens the life of dogwoods in the Gulf South's intense summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade; Cherokee Brave is the finest red-flowering dogwood cultivar — a selection with deep rosy-red to crimson-red bracts that are noticeably darker and more intensely colored than older red dogwood cultivars like 'Cherokee Chief.' The deep, saturated crimson-red bracts create one of the most spectacular spring flowering displays available for the partial-shade Southeast garden in March through April. Like the native species, Cherokee Brave is an understory tree — it evolved growing beneath the canopy of larger hardwoods in the forest edge, making it one of the finest specimens for sites that receive morning sun and afternoon shade. This understory adaptation is particularly valuable in zone 9 where the intense afternoon sun and summer heat stress full-sun dogwoods. The spring floral display is followed by attractive horizontal branching with dark-green summer foliage and excellent red fall color. Glossy red berries in fall provide bird food. In zone 9, dogwoods perform best in the northern portions and in protected, afternoon-shaded positions — mulch heavily around the root zone to maintain cool, moist soil. The striking contrast between the dark red bracts and white-centered bracts creates a bicolor effect that distinguishes Cherokee Brave from solid-red cultivars.

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container trees in late winter or early spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade; Appalachian Spring is the most disease-resistant Cornus florida cultivar — specifically selected for resistance to dogwood anthracnose (Discula destructiva), the devastating fungal disease that has killed millions of dogwood trees in the eastern US.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade; Appalachian Spring is the most significant disease-resistant flowering dogwood cultivar ever introduced — selected from wild dogwood populations in the Appalachian Mountains specifically for its outstanding resistance to dogwood anthracnose (Discula destructiva), the fungal disease that since the 1970s has killed millions of wild dogwood trees across the eastern United States and continues to devastate unresistant trees in humid forest conditions. Dogwood anthracnose causes branch dieback, water-soaked lesions, and can kill an entire tree within 2–3 years of infection — it is the primary reason dogwood populations have declined so dramatically in eastern American forests. Appalachian Spring was selected by the US National Arboretum through a program specifically aimed at finding anthracnose-resistant genotypes among surviving wild populations where the disease had been established for decades. The flower display is classic white Cornus florida — large, four-bracted, pure white flowers in March through April — combined with the full suite of ornamental qualities: excellent fall red color, attractive horizontal branching, and fall red berries for wildlife. In zone 9, Appalachian Spring performs similarly to other Cornus florida cultivars but offers insurance against the anthracnose that has become more of a concern in humid Gulf South conditions.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter through spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Carissa is one of the most compact and slow-growing Chinese holly cultivars — maintaining a mounded form of 3–4 feet without frequent pruning.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Carissa is a compact, slow-growing Chinese holly (Ilex cornuta) cultivar with a distinctive characteristic that sets it apart from all other Chinese holly cultivars: its leaves have only a single terminal spine rather than the multiple sharp spines of standard Chinese holly. This makes Carissa the most maintenance-friendly and user-safe holly cultivar for residential settings — plants around walkways, children's play areas, and high-traffic zones where the multiple sharp spines of Rotunda, Burford, and other Chinese holly types would create hazards. The deep, lustrous, dark-green foliage is more elongated and less glossy than some Chinese holly types but still attractive and evergreen year-round. Carissa is a female clone and requires a nearby male Chinese holly for berry production — when pollinated, it produces attractive red berries. The compact, naturally mounded habit (3–4 feet) requires minimal pruning to maintain in typical landscape situations. Extremely tough — tolerating clay soil, drought once established, heat, and partial shade without significant performance loss. Essentially pest-free, deer-resistant, and pollution-tolerant, making it practical for urban and suburban foundation plantings.

common_name: Rotunda Holly (Dwarf Chinese Holly, Mounding Chinese Holly)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter through spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Rotunda is a sterile male clone and does not produce berries — it is grown purely for its compact mounding form and dark-green foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Rotunda is one of the most widely planted compact foundation shrubs in the Gulf South — a male Chinese holly selection with a dense, rounded, mounding form that stays compact (2–4 feet tall, slightly wider) without the frequent pruning that many other Chinese holly cultivars require to maintain shape. Rotunda does not produce berries (it is a sterile male clone) and is grown purely for its clean, dark-green, heavily spined foliage and reliable, slow-growing mounded form. The extremely sharp, multiple-spined leaves make Rotunda an outstanding security planting under windows and along boundaries — the spines deter entry more effectively than most other barrier plants. Despite the spiny leaves, Rotunda is one of the most versatile, trouble-free foundation shrubs in the Gulf South: it tolerates heavy clay, drought, heat, partial shade, and the year-round humidity without significant pest or disease pressure. In typical residential foundation plantings, Rotunda maintains a clean, tight mound for years with minimal to no pruning — a significant advantage over faster-growing hollies that require aggressive trimming 2–3 times per season. Deer-resistant; essentially maintenance-free in well-drained soil.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter through spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Obsession thrives in partial shade in zone 9 — the bright red winter foliage color develops most vividly with some cold exposure, while full shade reduces the red intensity.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Obsession is a distinctive compact nandina cultivar selected for its exceptionally vivid and sustained red winter foliage color — the leaves turn deep fire-engine red in fall and winter, holding this brilliant color for months, creating one of the most eye-catching winter shrubs in the Gulf South landscape. Unlike Firepower nandina (which turns bright orange-red in full sun and greenish in shade), Obsession maintains its rich red color even in partial shade positions, expanding its landscape use to partially shaded areas where Firepower often reverts to less colorful foliage. Obsession additionally produces clusters of red berries — a quality that Firepower lacks — providing bird food and additional ornamental interest through winter. The compact, upright-mounding habit stays 2–3 feet tall and wide with minimal pruning. Like all nandinas, Obsession is remarkably tough — tolerating clay, drought, heat, humidity, and partial shade that challenge many other ornamental shrubs. Note: Nandina domestica and its berries are toxic to birds and the plant is considered invasive in some Gulf South natural areas (particularly the Southeast) — plant in contained landscape settings rather than adjacent to natural areas, and deadhead flowers if self-seeding becomes a concern.
